comments.text: I'm a Sucker for Lampreys (And You Should Be Too!), a comic I made about one of my favorite animals in 2024 and printed as a zine. (1/2)

This sucker is a sea lamprey—a parasite that can kill 40 pounds of fish in the Great Lakes in a year. My bill, the Great Lakes Fishery Research Reauthorization Act, would fund research to stop threats like sea lampreys from wreaking havoc on our native species. .bsky.social

Day 26 of Spooky Science 🧪 The lamprey’s mouth could easily be the cover of a horror film. The lamprey is an eel-shaped fish with a cartilage skeleton, belonging to a primitive group of jawless fishes called Agnathans. Learn more here: www.dec.ny.gov/animals/7242...
keeping this in mind for if any "Lamprey Eels Run Rampant In Great Lakes, Devastating Industry" headlines pop up

This week I learned how to tan fish skins into fish leather. I did some lake whitefish and sea lamprey. The yellow skin was dyed with turmeric. It was a very interesting process and something that I can see pursuing as a hobby and as a training offering for fisheries folks! #GreatLakes
This is…weird but the Trump administration cut programs that eradicated lampreys from the Great Lakes.

Last night I hung out with a bunch of Sea lampreys for a spooky science event at the Wisconsin Maritime Museum. Fun out reach and opportunity to stick lampreys to my arm! #GreatLakes fisheries have been impacted by this non-local being but control has reduced their numbers 90%.
Kidnapped these from the Great Lakes Aquarium. Who wants to snuggle my new pets?

Today's #FishOfTheWeek is the sea lamprey! You likely because they've become invasive in the Great Lakes, but there's WAY MORE to these fish than meets the eye. Like other lampreys, they have an extended juvenile phase, feeding on detritus for several years before switching to a diet of blood

Ugh...Sea lampreys...We have lampreys in the Great Lakes and have been fighting them for years...I've dived quite a bit...Everyone is afraid of sharks. Eels, lampreys, and sea snakes (and trigger fish) are what give me nightmares...

Every year, thousands of invasive sea lampreys migrate up the rivers around the North American Great Lakes. Recently, Griffin & co discovered that, unlike other fish, sea lampreys stick to the deepest part of the river to stay safe and use less energy journals.biologists.com/jeb/article/...

Vincent Bijman’s paper shows how the construction of the sea lamprey fish as a “vampire from the deeps” obscured broader environmental ills in the transborder American Great Lakes region, sparking trans-border cooperation, and flooding the water with a “lampricide”. OA: doi.org/10.1093/jhma...
Walking into an Great Lakes invasives conference room with lamprey leather pants would be the ultimate power move 😄

Sea lampreys negatively impact the ecological and economic health of the Great Lakes region. With support from the Great Lakes Fisheries Commission, researchers aim to improve sea lamprey traps that will remove these parasitic fish. View the #DeepBlueData here: doi.org/10.7302/py8y... #AcademicSky

So many anglers in the Great Lakes region are shocked to hear that not all lampreys are invasive.

The Florida panther, whooping crane and manatee shouldn’t be mourned. Like the bald eagle, they’re not great survivors. I’m told there are giant lampreys in Lake Superior the size rail cars. They’re even carnivorous. Here is the new icon of America’s greatness. The lamprey. Long may it suck.
Yes! Take the lampreys from the Great Lakes! Please! I’ve seen salmon from Lake Michigan with lamprey wounds. It isn’t pretty. Prognatho-Mom has seen them let go of fish that are being reeled in.

I thought of this analogy after watching a documentary on how sea lamprey killed off trout in the Great lakes: Trumpers are the sea lampreys of democracy. They attach themselves to the host (government), gradually bore a hole into the host and suck out the lifeblood until the host weakens and dies.

They are, but unfortunately we could lose them if the US administration does not keep funding the cross border collaborative science work that keeps them free of algae blooms and invasive lampreys. We must fight for our shared Great Lakes!

I think we need to be encouraging a broader segment of research to participate in the Science Homecoming. It's easy to defend cancer research or efforts to prevent the lamprey eel from decimating fish stocks in the great lakes.

The Great Lakes have an infestation of lampreys and they’re fucking delicious but most of the lakes are too fucked up to actually eat lampreys from even if you can fish safely because lampreys condense pollutants, it’ll be a minute still before lampreys are edible again

Northern brook lamprey. Unlike sea lamprey, these little guys are vegetarians and pose no threat to fish. But lampricide kills them too, so TBFN is working with Dept of Fisheries and Oceans to keep it from reaching up rivers and streams that empty in to Lake Superior.

Recent layoffs could allow over 1 million sea lampreys to survive, consuming nearly 5 million pounds of fish and causing $95 million in lost economic output. This threatens the $7 billion Great Lakes fishery, as previous reductions in control efforts led to a 300% increase in lamprey populations.

I've seen the teams spreading lampricide (a chemical used to kill the larvae of lampreys in rivers and streams. It's the primary method of controlling sea lampreys in the Great Lakes) on streams on the west coast of the Bay of Green Bay for years.
